title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
author: Rolley Haggard
orientation: Rolley Haggard uses the historical contrast between Neville Chamberlain and Winston Churchill to illustrate the strategic chasm between pro-life incrementalism and abolitionist immediatism, arguing that similar ends do not justify compromised means in the war against abortion.
audiences: history-minded reader interested in WWII analogies
pastor weighing public witness on abortion

questions.question: Why does the author reject the common claim that abolitionists and pro-lifers ultimately want the same thing?
questions.answer: Because the two camps actually pursue legally different ends: abolitionists demand that preborn children receive equal protection and that abortion be classified as murder, whereas the Pro-Life Establishment wants abortion treated as a lesser crime and accepts denying preborn children equal protection.
questions.quote: Abolitionists want preborn children treated as our equals under the law; we want abortion treated as murder. The Pro-Life Establishment wants abortion classified as a lesser crime, and for preborn children to be denied the equal protection of the law.

questions.question: Even granting that both sides wanted the same thing, why would shared goals still not justify shared strategies?
questions.answer: Because identical objectives do not require identical methods; some strategies for reaching a goal succeed and others ruin it, so the means must be evaluated on their own merits.
questions.quote: But clearly, similar ends imply neither the necessity nor the propriety of employing similar means to accomplish those ends. Some strategies are good, others are disastrous.

questions.question: How does the Chamberlain-Churchill comparison frame the difference between the two abortion strategies?
questions.audience: history-minded reader interested in WWII analogies
questions.answer: Both Chamberlain and Churchill said they wanted peace, but their methods diverged absolutely: Chamberlain compromised, conceded, and appeased Hitler, while Churchill defied him, issued ultimatums, and stood on principle even at the cost of war.
questions.quote:
Where Chamberlain would compromise with Hitler, Churchill would defy him. 

Where Chamberlain would issue concessions, Churchill would issue ultimatums. 

Where Chamberlain would appease, Churchill would stand on principle and, if necessary, go to war.

questions.question: How does the article characterize the incrementalist legislative approach?
questions.answer: Incrementalists are portrayed as appeasers who advance bills that ostensibly stop the killing of some preborn children while implicitly permitting in law the murder of all others, hoping the enemy will gradually relent.
questions.quote: Incrementalists (pro-lifers) are willing to meet the enemy halfway, appeasing him with concessionary proposals of legislation that ostensibly halt the murder of some preborn children while implicitly sanctioning in law the murder of all the rest. Gradually (so the thinking goes), the enemy will be worn down or come to his senses.

questions.question: What is the moral case Haggard makes for immediatism?
questions.audience: pastor weighing public witness on abortion
questions.answer: If abortion is murder, then any compromise is itself immoral; abolitionists must therefore demand the total and immediate criminalization of all abortion, since trading the protection of some lives for the continued killing of others is unacceptable.
questions.quote: Immediatists (abolitionists), on the other hand boldly assert that if abortion is murder (and it is), then compromise is immoral. All abortion ought, [on principle](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GBPV_ZFjtnY), to be immediately criminalized. It is wrong to attempt to purchase the supposed deliverance of some at the price of the continued murder of the rest.

questions.question: What does the author say is at stake if Christians fail to learn from the Chamberlain-Churchill lesson?
questions.audience: pastor weighing public witness on abortion
questions.answer: Failure to learn the lesson means disobeying God's prohibition of murder and ensuring continued failure, with the result being the perpetuation of mass abortion potentially without end.
questions.quote: To fail to glean from Chamberlain and Churchill lessons applicable to our own war against abortion is not only to disobey God (who prohibits all murder), but to actively court failure. Failure which, [as the past 49 years amply demonstrate](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C2XyfdMxyOc), equates to the continuation of the mass slaughter of the preborn, perhaps to the end of time.

questions.question: How does Haggard summarize the core distinction between the two movements in one image?
questions.answer: He boils the distinction down to a single comparison: pro-life incrementalism is Chamberlain and abolitionist immediatism is Churchill.
questions.quote: In a very real sense, the fundamental difference between incrementalism and immediatism (i.e.,  pro-lifeism and abolitionism) is hardly more complicated than this: the former is Chamberlain, the latter is Churchill.

questions.question: Does the Churchill analogy mean abolitionists endorse violence as a means of resistance?
questions.answer: No. The endnote clarifies that abolitionists explicitly reject violence; the analogy only illustrates that abolitionists will not back down from peaceable principled stands even when they provoke hostile opposition.
questions.quote: This is not to suggest that abolitionists promote or condone violence. We emphatically do not (see our [statement](https://abolishhumanabortion.com/abolitionism/why-abolition-must-be-non-violent/) on why abolitionism must be non-violent). The point is simply that we will not shrink from standing firm on the peaceable [principles](https://abolishhumanabortion.com/abolitionism/the-five-tenets-of-abolitionism/) of abolition, even if the price of fidelity to their practice is hostile opposition.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Direct expansion of the immediatist position that this article summarizes in nutshell form.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the concrete legislative critique of incrementalism that Haggard alludes to with his Chamberlain comparison.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Develops the moral argument that compromise legislation tacitly sanctions the murder of children not covered by the bill.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Engages prominent pro-life voices defending compromise, complementing this piece's appeasement framing.

related.title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
related.reason: Reinforces the Churchillian call to courageous principled stands rather than concessionary rhetoric.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Adds a theological dimension to the case against incrementalism that this nutshell article hints at when invoking God's prohibition of murder.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Movement-level statement of the immediatist principle from a sister site, useful as a primary-source companion.
